Nick Saban principle: "Don't look at the scoreboard. Play the next play."

The Expert Definition

"An expert is someone who's made every mistake possible in their field." — Niels Bohr

Bob Bowman: "I have a very narrow field, and I don't know if I've made every mistake possible, but I'm getting there very closely."

On Luck

"Luck is when preparation meets opportunity." — Bob Bowman

Michael Phelps: 8 Gold Medals (Beijing 2008)

When Michael walked out of the mix zone after winning the 8th gold medal, Bob didn't know what to say.

Bob: "Good job."

Michael later told USA Today: "All I had to do is win 8 gold medals to get a 'good job' from Bob. Nothing's ever good enough for him."

Bob's perspective: When you have somebody super talented, it's easy to do certain things. My job is to keep raising the bar.

"He'd swim 50 seconds and I'd say, '50's pretty good, but I bet if you took 2 strokes off and did 3 more kicks off that wall, you'd go 49.'"

Building the Toolbox

Early Career: Only Had a Hammer

"When I first started coaching, the only tool I had was a hammer, so everything looked like a nail."

"You do this. You WILL do this. Because I said so."

  • Incredibly effective
  • Wears you completely out
  • You cannot be other people's motivation. They have to motivate themselves.

Expanded Toolbox Now Includes:

  • Sledgehammer (when necessary)
  • Pat on the back
  • Logic
  • Empathy
  • Setting up a plan
  • Visualization guidance
  • Process focus

As mentors: Add to your toolbox. Different tools reach different people.

The Breakthrough Moment: Baltimore 1996

Bob was coaching in California, had two world-ranked swimmers who left him right before the 1996 Olympics.

Bob: "Screw this. I've done everything right. I've given up my whole life for this. I'm not ever going to get to the goals I wanted to. I'm going back to school and starting over."

Friend in Baltimore: "If you're going to go to school, come here. You can coach a little to make money."

Bob wanted to become a veterinarian. A year later, 11-year-old Michael Phelps showed up.

Bob's mindset: "I was kind of out the door. I said, 'I don't know if Michael can make it, but he's got more talent than anybody I've ever seen and I'm going to do everything I can in this year or 2 that I have with him to make him successful down the road.' I just took myself out of the equation because I never thought I'd ever coach him."

When Michael was 12-13, Bob got offered his dream job in Atlanta.

North Baltimore director: "This job in Atlanta is great. You could probably take it and do everything you want to do. But if you stay here, you'll be Michael's coach for the duration."

Bob stayed. "It just started to happen. That really wasn't part of the plan."

Create an Environment Where Success is Inevitable

"My coaching philosophy: I want to create an environment where success is inevitable. That if you're in the environment and you buy in and you do the things we ask you to do, you will be successful." — Bob Bowman

Application to Any Field

Everything Bob teaches applies beyond swimming:

  • Test anxiety? Focus on process (your preparation) not outcome (the grade)
  • Job interview? Rehearse specifics (your stories, questions) not worry ("What if I don't get it?")
  • Performance? Visualize it vividly, repeatedly, from multiple angles
  • Big project? Win = What's Important Now

Success is NOT a Straight Line

"For either Michael or myself, it wasn't just a straight line to success. You didn't just have an idea, a dream, and then just start working toward it and go directly toward it. Success has lots of ups, downs, backwards, forwards, and then over time what you hope to do is move in the direction where you want to be." — Bob Bowman

Links

  • Bob Bowman Overview
  • Coaching Philosophy
  • Michael Phelps Partnership
  • Leon Marchand Development
  • Goal Setting Systems
  • Visualization Techniques

articles.title: Bob Bowman: Leon Marchand Training & Methodology
articles.content:
# Bob Bowman: Leon Marchand Training & Methodology

*Source: Podcast transcripts 2024-2026*

## Coaching Leon vs. Michael: The Critical Difference

> "If I coached Leon like I coached Michael, he would quit in like two days. If I coached Michael like I coached Leon, he wouldn't have done anything near what he did." - Bob Bowman, Unfiltered Waters 2024

### Personality & Approach

**Michael Phelps**:
- Needed to get **really intense, almost angry** to perform best
- External motivation worked
- Pre-race: Stoke competitive fire, create enemies
- Example: "Michael Cavic said there's no way you should win. You're the one to stop yourself. Go."
- Thrived on pressure, conflict, challenge

**Leon Marchand**:
- Needs to be **really laid-back** to swim fast
- Gets worse when intense
- Pre-race: "Have fun out there"
- Indifferent to trash talk or external motivation
- Best when calm, relaxed, enjoying himself

**Bob's Adaptation**: "Every time I do something with him before races, it's just 'have fun out there, you know.' With Michael, I'd wind him up like a toy."

## Paris 2024 Olympics

### The Historic Performance

**Results**:
- 4 Gold Medals
- 1 Bronze Medal
- Multiple Olympic Records
- Home Olympics in Paris
- Defeated world record holders
- "A landmark Olympics at home, an event that will never be replicated"

### Pre-Olympics Preparation

**Bob's Concern (2023 Worlds)**:
- Leon won multiple golds at Fukuoka Worlds 2023
- Media obsession: "Will Leon break Michael's record?"
- Bob's connection to Leon became distraction for Team USA
- First question at press conference: "Bob, will Leon break Michael's record?"

**Decision**: Bob chose NOT to be on USA coaching staff for Paris 2024
- "My connection to him was really a distraction for Team USA"
- "If I had been wearing the USA shirt in the middle of all this stuff he was doing, it would just be a lot"
- Let Leon and France have their moment without Michael Phelps comparisons

### The Last Night Before Finals

**Bob's Warning**:
> "I wish we had more time to go over this, but things are not going to be the same for you. Just remember: stay connected to the people you know that are close to you and your family. Take a breath sometimes. We'll figure it out together."

Leon was about to be slammed.

## Post-Olympic Transition (2024-2025)

### The Problem: Post-Olympic Stress

**Bob's Historical Mistakes**:
> "I have poorly managed the period after the Olympics historically. I got it wrong with Michael like three times. Allison struggled."

**What Actually Happens**:
1. **Physical & Emotional Exhaustion** from the performance itself
2. **Celebration Period** (as it should be)
3. **But then**: Before you can even reflect on what happened...
   - Making big decisions about A LOT of money
   - Being pulled in 100 different directions
   - Mother signing autographs in the grocery store in Toulouse
   - Can't go anywhere in France without pictures/autographs
   - Complete loss of privacy (will never have it again)
   - New people entering your life
   - Agents, sponsors, media demands

**Bob's Realization**: "The time after is probably MORE stressful than the Olympics themselves for these swimmers."

### Leon's Specific Post-Olympic Reality

**In France**:
- Cannot go anywhere without being recognized
- Every public appearance = autographs, photos, conversations
- "A wonderful thing that they love him so much, but he has lost this level of privacy he'll probably never have again"
- Similar to Michael Phelps' experience

**Emotionally Exhausted**: By Short Course Worlds 2024 (December), Leon was drained

### The Transition Plan (Bowman's New Approach)

**Phase 1: Immediate Post-Olympics (July-August)**
- Time with family
- Celebration
- Processing what happened

**Phase 2: World Cup Tour (October)**
- Singapore, Shanghai, Incheon
- "Light training disguised as competition"
- Fun travel experience
- Kept him swimming without rigorous training demands
- Not competing in all events, selective schedule

**Phase 3: Short Break** (November)
- Skipped Short Course Worlds in Budapest (December)
- **Correct decision** according to Bob
- Needed more recovery time

**Phase 4: Australia with Dean Boxall** (December-February estimated)
- Training with Australian National Team
- Completely different environment
- New stimulus, new culture
- Dean is "an amazing coach, he'll learn some things, I'll get to learn some things"
- Different from Texas program structure

**Phase 5: Return to Austin** (Spring 2025 estimated)
- Fresh: mentally, physically, emotionally
- Ready to begin next Olympic quad
- Relative anonymity in Austin ("People care about football players, not swimmers")

**Full Program Timeline**: Not back to 100% until ~9-12 months post-Olympics

### Why Australia?

**Origin of Idea**:
- Leon did World Cup tour
- Connected with Italian swimmers also training with Dean Boxall
- They suggested it
- Leon approached Bob with the idea

**Bob's Initial Reaction**: "Well, you know, I don't know..."
- Coach's instinct: Get athletes back to training ASAP
- "I'm always itchy to get him back into things because I know the further you go, the further you go"

**Why Bob Said Yes**:
1. Leon was emotionally exhausted before Short Course Worlds
2. Needs something to refresh him
3. Needs something NOT in France/Europe
4. Needs to go far away
5. Then can return to Austin with relative anonymity
6. Similar to how Phoenix worked pre-Olympics

**Bob's Philosophy Shift**: "It just makes sense for him where he is right now."

### The "Inescapable Reality" Problem

**Leon's Training History with Bob**:
- 3 years in Bob's program (2021-2024)
- Led to historic Olympic performance
- But: "The inescapable reality of these training programs is they don't really change that much"
- Another 3+ years coming up of same program structure
- Risk of staleness, burnout

**Australia Solution**:
- Different stimulus
- Learn new things
- Mix it up
- Then return refreshed and ready for familiar program
- "He'll need that to make the next step"

## Training Methodology for Leon

### Emotional State Management

**Pre-Race Routine**:
- Keep him calm
- No competitive fire-stoking
- No creating enemies or challenges
- Simple: "Have fun out there"
- Trust his preparation

**Practice Environment**:
- Can't be relentlessly intense like with Michael
- Laid-back atmosphere
- Let him enjoy the process
- When he gets intense, performance suffers

### Physical Training (Limited Details in Transcripts)

**Implied Approach**:
- High-level professional training
- Texas program structure
- But delivered with different emotional energy than Michael era
- Same physical demands, different psychological climate

### The Core Group Concept

**Bob's Emphasis**: "Stay connected to the people who were there BEFORE success"

**Leon's Core Group**:
- Parents
- Nico Castelli (French coach)
- Bob Bowman
- Very trusted advisers
- Small, stable circle

**Why This Matters**:
- Post-Olympics, "a whole lot of new people come into your life that really weren't part of the process before"
- Core group provides stability
- Helps navigate new relationships (agents, sponsors, media)

## Comparison to Michael Phelps Journey

### Similarities

1. **Young talent identified early**
2. **Long-term development** (both 3+ years with Bob before major success)
3. **Historic Olympic performances**
4. **World record holder**
5. **Loss of privacy post-success**
6. **Media pressure and expectations**

### Differences

| Michael Phelps | Leon Marchand |
|----------------|---------------|
| American, Olympics abroad | French, home Olympics |
| Needed intensity/anger | Needs calm/fun |
| Thrived on conflict | Indifferent to conflict |
| Multiple Olympics from young age | Breakthrough at 22 |
| Baltimore → Ann Arbor → Phoenix | France → ASU → Texas |
| Bob's first superstar | Bob's second act |
| Post-Olympic management: mistakes | Post-Olympic management: corrected |

## Media & Pressure Management

### Fukuoka 2023 Worlds

**The Michael Phelps Shadow**:
- Every press conference: "Will Leon break Michael's records?"
- Bob's distraction from Team USA responsibilities
- Leon handling comparisons while trying to compete

### Paris 2024

**Bob's Decision**:
- Stayed away from Team USA coaching role
- Let Leon have his own story
- Avoided Phelps comparison narrative
- Allowed French national pride moment to be pure

**Result**: Leon's performance stood on its own merit

### Post-Olympics

**Different than Michael's Era**:
- Social media amplifies everything
- Instant global recognition
- 24/7 media cycle
- France's swimming boom (Leon as national hero)

**Bob's Learning**: Can't control media, but CAN control athlete's exposure and recovery time

## Looking Forward: Next Olympic Quad (2025-2028)

### Bob's Goals for Leon

**Indicated Priorities**:
1. **Can swim faster in all his events** (Bob believes this)
2. Avoid burnout through varied training stimuli
3. Maintain joy in the sport
4. Develop even more independence as professional
5. Continue technical refinement

**Not Stated Goal**: Recreate Paris 2024
- "He'll never have a time where he is at home, Olympic champion four times, defeating world record holders. And after, his life has changed forever."
- Next Olympics: Different context, different goals
- Process over outcome

### Keys to Success 2025-2028

**Based on Bob's Approach**:
1. **Extended recovery** (already doing)
2. **Varied training environments** (Australia, Texas, possibly others)
3. **Relative anonymity in Austin** (allows normal life)
4. **Core group stability** (family, Nico, Bob)
5. **Professional independence** (owns his own career)
6. **Fun as priority** (keeps performance optimal)
7. **Strategic competition schedule** (not everything, every time)

## Lessons from Leon for Coaches

### 1. Individualization is Non-Negotiable

- What worked for Michael would destroy Leon
- What works for Leon would have limited Michael
- Know your athlete's psychology

### 2. Post-Championship Management Matters

- The work doesn't end with the medal
- Plan for 6-12 month transition
- Protect athlete from exhaustion
- Allow space to be human

### 3. Environment Selection is Strategic

- Austin's relative anonymity = asset
- Australia's novelty = refreshment
- France's adoration = overwhelming
- Choose training location for mental health, not just physical facilities

### 4. Long-term > Short-term

- Bob could have pushed Leon back to training immediately
- Would have been physically fine
- Would have been mentally damaging
- Patience now = longevity later

### 5. Core Group Stability

- Keep tight circle during chaos
- New people will come
- Old people provide grounding
- Trust the ones who were there before success

## Bob's Growth as a Coach (Through Leon)

**What Leon Has Taught Bob**:
1. Patience with post-Olympic transitions
2. Value of varied training environments
3. Calm > intensity for some athletes
4. Importance of fun for optimal performance
5. When to let go and trust the process

**Bob's Reflection**:
> "Hopefully we learn, right?"

## Summary: The Leon Marchand Method

1. **Keep it fun** - performance follows joy
2. **Stay calm** - intensity hurts his swimming
3. **Extended post-championship recovery** - 9-12 months transition
4. **Varied environments** - Australia, Austin, strategic planning
5. **Core group stability** - family, Nico, Bob
6. **Process focus** - not recreating past glories
7. **Strategic anonymity** - training in places where he can be normal
8. **Professional independence** - he owns his career decisions
9. **Long-term vision** - another 3+ years, sustainable approach
10. **Trust the athlete** - he knows when he needs what

**Bottom Line**: Leon isn't Michael 2.0. He's Leon 1.0. And Bob has learned how to coach him accordingly.

articles.title: Bob Bowman: Lessons from Michael Phelps
articles.content:
# Bob Bowman: Lessons from Michael Phelps

*Source: Multiple transcripts (2024-2026)*

## The Four Factors of Excellence

**From "What Swimmers Miss in Freestyle" transcript:**

### 1. Physiological Makeup
> "He's made for swimming. There's no question about it. He has genetic gifts for swimming. So that was a win."

**Physical advantages**:
- Arm span
- Torso length
- Flexible joints
- Large hands/feet
- Ideal body composition

**Lesson for coaches**: You can't create this, but you can maximize whatever genetics your athlete has.

### 2. Environment (North Baltimore Aquatic Club)
> "He happened to be at North Baltimore Aquatic Club where there is a history of producing Olympic swimmers and world records and the expectations were there."

**Environmental factors**:
- Culture of excellence
- Peer excellence (others going to Olympics)
- High expectations as norm
- "Little kids like, 'Oh, I'm going to do that'"

**Lesson**: Environment shapes what athletes believe is possible.

### 3. Mindset & Work Ethic

**The Christmas Story**:
- National team trip, older swimmers: "Phelps, I bet you train every day. Do you train every day?"
- Michael: "Yeah." (didn't know any different)
- "Do you even train on Christmas?"
- Michael: "Yeah."
- Bob: "On Christmas, literally, they came in and swam for 30 minutes. We played water polo. It wasn't like a big deal."

**The Psychological Advantage**:
> "The guys gave him this look that I knew was a huge psychological advantage. Michael's like, 'We're going to keep doing this.' They were then terrified of him at that point."

**The Math**:
- 52 extra days per year
- Over 6 years = 312 extra days
- ~10+ months of additional training
- "And you know, you can make the case... but it's how it kind of became."

### 4. Willingness to Learn
> "An emotional willingness to learn. That's the thing about Michael even today. Every night I go over there for dinner pretty much, and Michael cooks dinner most nights and he is constantly wanting to learn how to do it better."

**The Steak Example**:
- "Should I cook these steaks five and a half minutes on each side or five or what do you think?"
- After serving: "Wow, that's really great."
- Michael: "No, I should have probably gone four, four and a half."

**Constant Improvement**:
> "He's constantly trying to raise his game and learn about whatever he's doing. And he was like that in swimming. There's no better student of the sport in general than Michael."

---

## Practice Philosophy Lessons

### Consistency Over Perfection

**The "8 out of 10" Principle**:
> "Both of them [Michael & Leon] are capable of doing extraordinary things in practice when they're completely focused and they're physically right. But what I found is they very rarely give you like a 10 out of 10 practice, but almost every practice is an eight."

**Key Insight**: "Have a very high average level of performance. I think that's what's the most important thing."

**Application**:
- Don't chase perfect practices
- Aim for consistently excellent practices
- "Over time, if you're throwing in eights day in day out, this is going to be a significant way to improve."

### Value of Individual Practice

**Both Michael & Leon**:
- "Big goals that are quite specific"
- "Broken them into plan pieces that we diligently work toward every day"
- "Both recognize the value of an individual practice"
- "Want to get as much as possible out of each practice"

**Mindset**: "Sometimes it's a lot, sometimes it's not that much, but they almost never leave the pool thinking that they went backwards."

**They found some way to get something out of that practice.**

### Small Bites of Big Goals

**How to Eat an Elephant**:
> "You can achieve any goal if you make the first step small enough. You don't have to worry about eight gold medals. What you have to worry about is this 500 free that's right in front of your nose right now. Do the turns better. There's something small that they can do."

**The Process**:
1. Big goal exists (8 gold medals)
2. Break into smaller goals (each race)
3. Break into practice components (turns, technique)
4. Focus on what you control TODAY

> "How do you eat an elephant? One bite at a time. It's just one little bite. You don't just do the whole thing."

### Control Philosophy

**What You Can't Control**:
- Tomorrow's conditions
- 4 years from now
- Weather, pool conditions
- How you'll feel

**What You CAN Control**:
> "But what you do have pretty good control of is what's going to happen in the next two hours."

**Focus**: The practice you're in, RIGHT NOW.

---

## The Genetics vs. Work Debate

### What Was Given vs. What Was Earned

**Bob's Honest Assessment**:
- Michael had genetic advantages
- Environment was ideal
- BUT...

**The Differentiator**:
1. **Willingness to work** when others didn't
2. **Consistency** over years and years
3. **Learning mindset** never stopped
4. **Attention to detail** (perfect finishes, turns, technique)

**The 0.01 Second** (vs. Milorad Čavić, 2008 Beijing):
- Genetic gifts didn't win that race
- 12 years of yelling about finishes won that race
- "Do it over. That finish was terrible. Make sure you're on a straight arm."
- Ingrained muscle memory under pressure

---

## Competition Mindset Lessons

### The "Can't Stand to Lose" Mentality

**Comparison with Other Greats**:
> "Michael and Kobe and Michael Jordan and Tiger... they cannot stand to lose. They can't tolerate losing. It's not that they love winning. The winning is great, but that's what they feel like they're supposed to do."

**The Motivation Cycle**:
- Lose once → cannot tolerate it
- Will do "whatever it takes" to not get beat next time
- Winning = expected outcome, not emotional high
- Losing = unacceptable, emotional motivator

**Application**: Different athletes need different motivation. Michael needed competitive fire. Leon needs calm.

### Pre-Race Mental State

**Michael's Philosophy** (from Bob):
> "I've done everything I can do. All I'm going to do is just give my best. I know that I've prepared. We'll see how well the preparation went. That's when you find out."

**Process Focus**:
- Can't control outcome
- CAN control preparation
- Race = test of preparation
- Result = feedback on process

### Sleep as Superpower

**Michael's Sleep Ability**:
- Could sleep well even before major competitions
- Bob: "Michael was a very good sleeper"
- "I could just look in his eyes and know if he was rested"

**2000 Olympic Trials Story** (Michael age 15):
- Bob (nervous): "Did you get any sleep?"
- Michael: "Yeah, three and a half hours."
- Bob: "All right, we're good."

**Ability to perform on minimal sleep** = huge competitive advantage

**Leon's Similar Trait**: "If he gets his sleep, sleep is his superpower. The only time Leon's ever really compromised in training is when for some reason, as upset as sleep."

---

## Raising the Bar Philosophy

### Never Satisfied (In a Good Way)

**The "Good Job" Story** (2008 Beijing, after 8 gold medals):
- Bob didn't know what to say
- Michael came through media zone
- Bob: "Good job."
- Michael later told USA Today: "Yeah, all I had to do is win 8 gold medals to get a good job from Bob. Nothing's ever good enough for him."

**Bob's Response**: "That's part of the mindset, right? When you've got somebody that's super talented, it's easy to do certain things. My job is to keep raising the bar."

**The Continuous Improvement Cycle**:
- Athlete swims 50 seconds
- Coach: "50's pretty good, but I bet if you took 2 of the last strokes and you did 3 more kicks off that wall, you'd go 49."
- Already started process toward next thing

**Application**: "That's just like you guys. Whatever you're doing. Made a B on this test, but if I really got this part down, I could make an A next time."

---

## Technical Excellence Lessons

### Importance of Technique

**From transcripts**: "They both realize the importance of technique and they're trying to improve it all the time."

**Even at Elite Level**:
- Michael constantly working on details
- Never "good enough"
- Every repeat = chance to practice perfect technique
- Details matter more than people think

### The Details Story (Butterfly Finish)

**Proper finish**: Fingers extended, horizontal plane, drive straight into wall

**12 Years of Practice**:
- "Do it over. That finish was terrible."
- "Make sure you're on a straight arm."
- Ingrained through thousands of repetitions
- Can't make decision in the moment → rely on muscle memory

**Result**: 0.01 second victory, changed both their lives

**Bob's Point**: "I bought a 37-acre horse farm and built a 5,000 square foot house off of that 100th of a second. I promise you it mattered."

---

## Practice Structure Insights

**Bowman's Structure** (applied with Michael):

### Warm-up: ~30% of practice
- Gradually warm joints and shoulders
- Reinforce good technique
- Get mentally ready for main set
- Might include: swimming, kicking, pulling, drilling

### Main Set: ~50% of practice
- Primary objective for the day
- Energy system specific
- Stroke specific
- Event specific
- Alternating system through week

### Post-Main: ~20% of practice
- Series of sprints
- Technical work
- Long recovery swim

**Example Week Pattern**:
- Different energy systems each day
- Threshold, aerobic, anaerobic, mix
- Stroke variety
- Event specificity

---

## Mental Training Lessons

### Dealing with Adversity

**The Do Your Work Mantra**:
> "What do you do when you've never felt better in your life when you come to practice? Do your work. You feel terrible and you can't... you're not motivated and you just don't know what to do? Do your work. If you're uncertain, do your work."

**Philosophy**: "These incremental, just consistently, mind-numbingly boring, consistent every day, you know, deposits in your bank account."

### Overcoming Setbacks

**2008: Broken Hand** (6 months before Beijing):
- Sunday morning call: Michael broke hand slipping on ice
- Choice: 6 weeks in cast OR surgery + 10 days to swimming
- Decision: "The surgery is tomorrow"
- Adapted training, continued preparation
- Still achieved 8 gold medals

**Lesson**: Adversity happens. Adapt and keep moving forward.

---

## The Grandfather Effect

**Bob's Evolution Through Michael's Kids**:
- Boomer, Beckett, Maverick (Michael's sons)
- "Grandpa Bob" (started as joke, became real)

**Impact on Coaching**:
> "Whenever I think I'm going to start getting pretty hard on somebody, I picture Boomer. I talk to them like I would talk to Boomer. I also talk to myself like I would talk to them."

**Before**: Hammer approach, relentless criticism
**Now**: Empathy, patience, love

**Quote on empathy**: Current focus as coach - "try to have it, try to live it. Not only for others but also for myself."

---

## Goal Setting: Michael's Journey

**Age 11 Dream Goal**: "Swim in the Olympics"
- On back of paper (crossed out): "I want to win a gold"
- Too big to write down, but already in his mind

**20 Year Process**:
- 11 years old: Dream to make Olympics
- 15 years old: Made Olympic team (2000 Sydney)
- 19 years old: First gold medal (2004 Athens)
- 23 years old: 8 gold medals (2008 Beijing)
- Multiple comebacks and retirements
- 31 years old: Final Olympics (2016 Rio)

**2008 Meeting** (before Beijing):
- Team asked: What do you want us to say about expectations?
- Got quiet
- Michael: **"I want to be the best ever."**

**Bob's Reaction**: "I have never believed anything more in my life than those words when he said it. I had 100% conviction that he could do that and we would do that."

---

## Key Lessons Summary

### For Athletes
1. **Consistency > perfection** (8/10 every day)
2. **Small bites** (break big goals into daily tasks)
3. **Value each practice** (never leave thinking you went backward)
4. **Willingness to learn** (always looking to improve)
5. **Can't control tomorrow** (control the next 2 hours)
6. **Do your work** (show up, regardless of feelings)
7. **Details matter** (12 years of finishes = 0.01 second)

### For Coaches
1. **Environment matters** (culture shapes belief)
2. **Keep raising the bar** (talent makes things easy, your job is to push)
3. **Know your athlete** (Michael ≠ Leon in approach needed)
4. **Long-term vision** (20 year journey from age 11)
5. **Adapt to setbacks** (broken hand didn't end the dream)
6. **Empathy evolved** (Grandpa Bob vs. young coach Bob)
7. **Process over outcome** (prepare perfectly, let results come)

### Universal Principles
1. **Genetics + Environment + Work + Learning = Excellence**
2. **High average > occasional peaks**
3. **Daily deposits in the bank**
4. **Control what you can control**
5. **Never stop learning and improving**

**The Michael Phelps story isn't just about talent. It's about what happens when talent meets perfect environment, relentless work ethic, and willingness to learn for 20 years straight.**

articles.title: Bob Bowman: Texas NCAA Championship 2025
articles.description: *Source: SwimSwam Podcast with Coleman Hodges - Post-NCAA Championship Interview*
articles.content:
# Bob Bowman: Texas NCAA Championship 2025

*Source: SwimSwam Podcast with Coleman Hodges - Post-NCAA Championship Interview*

**Historic Achievement:** First coach to win back-to-back NCAA team titles with two different teams (ASU 2024, Texas 2025)

---

## The Integration Strategy: How to Win Year One

### Gradual Implementation (60-70% → 100%)

Bob didn't come in and blow up everything. **He started at 60-70% of his system and gradually built up.**

**What They Kept (Texas Traditions):**
- Friday clap meeting
- Team dinner every Friday
- Dress up for NCAAs + cowboy hats
- Long-standing Texas cultural elements

**What They Changed:**
- Practice structure (everything on paper when you arrive)
- Set-to-set rest time (shortened dramatically)
- Strength training protocols
- Fall morning volume (7,000-8,000 yards in 90-100 minutes)

### The Transition Team

**Key people who bridged the gap:**
1. **Hubert Kós** - Knew Bob's system, could explain the "why" behind workouts
2. **Chase Kalisz** - Training as a pro at Texas, helped younger swimmers understand expectations
3. **Senior leadership** - Bought in first, everyone else followed

---

## NCAA Teams vs. International: Different Math

### International Preparation
- All about YOUR time
- Hit specific time at specific competition
- Individual focus

### NCAA Team Championship
- **It's a numbers game** - scoring points, not just swimming fast
- Everybody plays a role
- **Texas wouldn't have won without diving**
- **Texas wouldn't have won without ALL the swimmers who scored**

**Holistic experience:**
- Perform under pressure
- Go above expectations
- Come back from setbacks
- Grind it out when it's not going your way

---

## The Meet: How Texas Won

### Seeding Situation
Seeded to score 200+ points more than next team (similar to ASU 2024)

**Bob's confidence came from:**
- Swam well at SECs on NOT a full taper
- Top 4 scorers (Kós, Luke Hobson, Rex Maurer, etc.) were **unshaved at SECs**
- Knew they had room to improve

### Why Bob Gave Them More Rest Earlier

**Bob gave more taper for:**
1. **Fall invite** - needed to see where they were
2. **Indiana dual meet** - needed confidence boost

**The difference from ASU:**
- ASU was confident but inexperienced at NCAAs
- Texas had gotten 7th the previous year
- Eddie Reese told Bob at breakfast: **"I'm so sorry. They're way better than that."**
- Texas has to walk the hallways and see championship banners every day

> "I was trying to coach them mentally as well as physically. If I just did a straight physiological plan, our invite I'd probably just rolled through it with most people, but I felt like we needed to see where they were and have them feel good about what we were doing so far." — Bob Bowman

### Indiana Dual Meet: Going to the Well?

**Ray Looze (Indiana coach):** "You can only go to the well so many times."

**Bob's response:** They got TWO DAYS of fresher training before Indiana. Bob's philosophy: **"I don't see the point in competing so far away from where you're going to be."**

- Warmed up the day before
- No lifting (because Indiana flew in, so they didn't lift either)
- "We saw the well over there somewhere. I don't know. You know what I mean? We kind of saw it."

Result: Way better than Bob expected.

> "What's the point of swimming a 500 fifteen seconds slower than your best time? Maybe 5 or 10 seconds is good. But Michael [Phelps] was always relatively close to his speed and we trained 80,000 a week. When we went to a meet, we didn't just keep that going till the day we got there. It seems stupid to do that. Why would we even go? Just keep practicing." — Bob Bowman

---

## The Championship Meet: Day-by-Day

### Night 1: Amazing
Exceeded expectations. Very good.

**Texas's advantage:** First four events are their best events.

### Day 2 Morning: Body Blows
- 200 medley relay DQ in the final (every point counts)
- Taking punches, just trying to hang in
- **But somehow still increased the lead slightly**

### Day 3 Morning (Last Prelims): Struggle
"We didn't swim as well as we needed to. A lot of that's inexperience. A lot of that was they'd given a lot in the first three days."

**Bob's response:** Gave them an **objective assessment** of what they needed to do.

### The Emotional Control Lesson

Bob's first inclination: "All right, come on, we gotta—"

**But that's wrong.** They're already feeling the pressure. They don't need MORE pressure from the coach.

> "The reality is if we want them to perform, they're already feeling the pressure. They don't need pressure from me. I need to take it away if anything. I need to mitigate it so they can perform at their best." — Bob Bowman

**The seniors had a team meeting alone after Night 3.** Whatever they said worked. The team hung in there.

> "The team very much like the ASU team kind of motivated itself. We didn't have to give them too much." — Bob Bowman

### Night 3 (Finals): The Comeback

**What Texas needed:**
- A few people move up 3-5 places
- Get point swings in their direction

**Key performances:**
1. **Luke Hobson** - Moved up to 9th in 100 breast (substantial move)
2. **Nate Germonprez** - Went from lane 1 to **winning the consolation final** (biggest difference maker)
3. **Jacob Welch** - Moved up in platform diving (weren't sure they'd have a platform diver)

**The 400 Free Relay:**
- Texas had to finish top 12
- Had to be within a certain margin of Cal
- Bob: "I would have liked to have had it done before the relay. At ASU we did that, then you can send them and they can do amazing times. But in our situation, we had to have everybody go."

**Relay starts:** "I said, be deliberate. Maybe not THAT deliberate, but it was great."

Result: **Texas wins NCAA Championship**

---

## Building Discomfort Tolerance

### Low-Hanging Fruit Strategy

Bob immediately looked for ways to make them **uncomfortable**, then make them deal with it.

### Example 1: Practice Pace

**Eddie Reese's system:**
- Decide what he was going to do
- Tell group what they're doing
- Go to next lane, deliver to them
- Amazing practices, but **lots of time between sets**

**Bob Bowman's system:**
- Everything on paper when you arrive
- Between sets: **about 1 minute**
- "Sometimes I try to make it less. They finish one set, I'll be like 'On the top, start the pulling.'"

**Swimmers' reaction:** "Completely flummoxed in the beginning."

But that alone put them in an uncomfortable state. After a few days, they adapted.

### Example 2: Fall Morning Volume

**Bob's favorite fall routine:**
- Build general aerobic work (kicking, pulling, swimming)
- Short rest
- **7,000-8,000 yards in 90-100 minutes**

"They're just completely not used to it, not really comfortable with it in the beginning, but after a while they'd say, 'Oh yeah, I can do this.' And that made them more confident."

### Example 3: The Christmas Sledgehammer

**After Christmas break, long course meters:**

- 10x100 freestyle from a dive, on 2:00 (maximal effort)
- Straight into 10x50 fly from a dive, on 1:30 (maximal effort)
- Everyone climbs out after every one
- **Everyone does it** - sprinters, distance, doesn't matter
- Coaches yelling and screaming: "Come on, you can do it!"

Guys got into it. "Wow, I don't even know if I can do this."

Then they do it.

> "That muscle starts to get bigger. They get a little more strong in the brain. I was reading where there's a part of your brain that when you get yourself to do something you either don't want to do or don't think you can do, every time you do that a little area of your brain grows and gets better at it the next time." — Bob Bowman

---

## On "Going to the Well"

Coleman: "I love that sound clip of Ray [Looze] saying 'you can only go to the well so many times.'"

Bob: **"I think your number is maybe higher than his."**

> "The point's well taken. It all depends on what that means. It means we just don't want to have them absolutely so dead they can't move their arms? Yeah, I guess we do that. I just don't see the point in competing so far away from where you're going to be. What's the point of swimming a 500 fifteen seconds slower than your best time? Maybe 5 or 10 is good, there's something in there. I don't think you have to do your best time all the time, but I've always felt that even in my club days, Michael was always relatively close to his speed." — Bob Bowman

---

## The Hubert Kós Story

### SECs: Lost Every Event
Got beat in every event at SECs (unshaved).

### NCAAs: Dominant
**SEC Swimmer of the Year** but **All-SEC Second Team** (because you have to WIN an event to make first team).

Bob: "It's pretty cool. Keeps him humble. Keep working."

### The 200 Back: 1:34-low

**Coleman:** "The swim of the meet to me."

**Bob:** "He was mad he didn't go 1:33. We had talked about going 1:33."

**The setup:**
- Week before NCAAs, Bob does suited swims (broken swim or effort)
- Kós swam 150 back: **1:10.0**
- Exactly what he split at NCAAs: **1:10.0**
- After that 150, Kós: "Man, I could go like 1:33 or something."
- Bob: "Yeah, feel free. Go ahead. Do it. Please do."

**Bob's assessment:** "He's capable of that for sure."

### Long Course: "He's got a lot of work to do"

"He's far away [from world-class long course times], so we need to start dropping this stuff by a lot."

### Hungarian Nationals (Spring 2025)

Kós was sick before the meet. Bob had no idea how he'd do.

**Result:** Great. First day he swam 5 times (relay + individual events). All of it pretty good.

**Bob's takeaway:** "That tells me he was in good condition right at NCAAs. Maybe he could have rested more for NCAAs."

**Two-year progression:**
- 2024 (ASU): Bob "really blew it with him" - let him get way too tired, shaved him for Pac-12s (should NOT have done that)
- 2025 (Texas): **Got it right** - swam SECs, got beat in every event, then peaked perfectly for NCAAs

---

## Long Course Transition

### Start Long Course IMMEDIATELY After Short Course

"They're already in shape. We just roll right into it."

**2025 Timeline:**
- Trials: June 3
- ~40 days away (at time of interview)
- Serious trials athletes: **4 days off, then back in full training**

"Use the speed you have and roll that into the next training phase."

### Long Course During College Season

**Tuesday/Thursday/Saturday long course** most of the time (maybe not the last month before championships).

**After NCAAs:**
- Add Monday afternoons
- Tuesday morning
- Thursday morning
- Saturday morning
- Add Wednesday
- **At least 5 long course sessions** per week

### Bob's Philosophy on Long Course Training

> "Excellence in long course is about your fitness level and your technical level, and there are many ways to get that. I don't think it's just swimming long course every time because if you swim long course all the time, they just get so sloggy and it's hard to get speed. I kind of keep a good mix. I want them to have enough long course that they know the pace and the stroke counts and those things and they get the conditioning to do it, but I also like to keep enough short course in there so they maintain their speed and their strokes are just better in the short course." — Bob Bowman

### Short Course Meters

**At NBAC (North Baltimore):** Did it all the time in the outdoor pool.

**Benefits:**
- "Nobody knows what they're doing, so it's kind of a great relief."
- By the time you figure out the intervals, it's over
- Swim things similar to long course (200 SCM is way faster than 200 LCM, but still meters)
- **Varies the stimulus**
- Swimmers get a little confidence

---

## On Leon Marchand's Return

**Injuries:**
- Rib injury (Bob knew about, stayed in contact)
- Shoulder injury (news to Bob - "I think he kept that one to himself")

**Competition schedule leading to French Trials:**
1. Fort Lauderdale Pro Swim
2. Texas elite meet (2 weeks later) - added specifically because college guys can't do Fort Lauderdale due to exams
3. Trials (2 weeks after that)

**Fort Lauderdale:** Leon will swim some freestyle events.

**Bob on Leon's needs:** "He needs to get his timing back."

---

## On Olympic 50s of Stroke

**Bob's position:** Fine with the events themselves, but **watch the roster implications.**

**Concerns:**
- Hard to cover all those events
- **Relay alternates probably won't happen anymore** (Bob's okay with this)
- Need a good selection plan
- Need to think about how it affects relays

**Benefits:**
- Exciting events
- Big population of world swimmers can participate
- USA should excel in these

### Shane Casas and the 50s

**When 50s were announced:**

Shane texted Bob immediately: "50s! 🎉"

Bob: "It's awesome. We got a set this afternoon. It's going to get you ready for these 50s."

Shane: All pumped.

**The set:** 3,000 straight (mixed up stuff, basically a straight 3K)

**After practice text from Shane:** "Uh, very funny."

Bob: "You know, I try. Come on, Shane. Give me a break."

**Bob:** "He's really excited. I know a lot of people are excited about it. I'm sure it'll keep some people swimming longer than they probably would have before."

**Coleman:** "I thought Shane was training for the 400 IM."

**Bob:** "Well, that's what he will tell you. Maybe the 200 IM is in there somewhere. But he's actually doing great. So fun to work with."

---

## The Benefits of Racing

> "I like that we have more racing now. I think I like that you see more coaches adopting that philosophy of staying sharp and racing more. You get some benefits from racing you don't get from training, in all kinds of ways—psychological, physiological, all of those things are there. And it's kind of fun. Let's face it, the majority of stuff we do is not that fun. Trust me, I've been doing it for a long time. You just watch them going up and down all the time." — Bob Bowman

**2025 Worlds issue:** 6-7 weeks between trials and Worlds.

**Bob's solution:** "We have to have something that's like a meet, whether we just make it our own or we go somewhere. There has to be a competition in there. That's a long time."

---

## Coaching Evolution: The Blowup That Saved a Medal

**2024 Paris Olympics - Chase Kalisz, 400 IM:**

Chase had been with Bob since age 7. Michael Phelps was his role model the whole way.

For a long time, Chase "had not been doing it right."

**Bob dropped a nuclear bomb on him about a flip turn.**

> "I know I seem really sweet and nice as I talk to you today, but I can get a little bit forceful." — Bob Bowman

Bob thought Chase was actually going to leave.

**Michael Phelps called Chase:**
> "Look, I know Bob is hard on you. It's only because he wants you to succeed. And if you don't change that turn, you don't have a chance to win a medal in the Olympic Games." — Michael Phelps

Chase came back. **Never did a bad turn again.**

**Paris Olympics 400 IM:** Chase wins silver.

**First thing Chase said after:** "You know what? That turn really saved me about 5-10ths. That's why I won the silver medal."

---

## Tower Lighting: The Texas Tradition

Monday night after winning NCAAs in Seattle, Texas did the **tower lighting** on campus.

> "You know, to be able to go in my office at the pool and put up a picture of one of our championship teams to go with the many that are already there, that was pretty special." — Bob Bowman

---

## The Roster

**Bob on his pro group:**
- Hubert Kós (working on long course, "far away" from world-class LCM times)
- Leon Marchand (returning from rib/shoulder injuries)
- Shane Casas (training for 50s, "maybe 400 IM, maybe 200 IM")
- Chase Kalisz (training as pro, mentoring college team)
- College roster: Many sophomores scored major points

**Bob's assessment:** "In general, our team is relatively young. A lot of the points were scored by sophomores."

---

## Links

- [Bob Bowman Overview](./README.md)
- [Golden Rules](./golden-rules.md)
- [Coaching Philosophy](./coaching-philosophy.md)
- [ASU 2024 NCAA Championship](/wiki/programs/asu-2024.md)
- [Hubert Kós Development](../athletes/hubert-kos.md)
- [Discomfort Training](/wiki/methodology/discomfort-training.md)
- [Taper and Peaking Strategies](/wiki/methodology/taper-peaking-strategies.md)

The Integration Strategy: How to Win Year One

Gradual Implementation (60-70% → 100%)

Bob didn't come in and blow up everything. He started at 60-70% of his system and gradually built up.

What They Kept (Texas Traditions):

  • Friday clap meeting
  • Team dinner every Friday
  • Dress up for NCAAs + cowboy hats
  • Long-standing Texas cultural elements

What They Changed:

  • Practice structure (everything on paper when you arrive)
  • Set-to-set rest time (shortened dramatically)
  • Strength training protocols
  • Fall morning volume (7,000-8,000 yards in 90-100 minutes)

The Transition Team

Key people who bridged the gap:

  1. Hubert Kós - Knew Bob's system, could explain the "why" behind workouts
  2. Chase Kalisz - Training as a pro at Texas, helped younger swimmers understand expectations
  3. Senior leadership - Bought in first, everyone else followed

NCAA Teams vs. International: Different Math

International Preparation

  • All about YOUR time
  • Hit specific time at specific competition
  • Individual focus

NCAA Team Championship

  • It's a numbers game - scoring points, not just swimming fast

  • Texas wouldn't have won without diving
  • Texas wouldn't have won without ALL the swimmers who scored

  • Perform under pressure
  • Go above expectations
  • Come back from setbacks
  • Grind it out when it's not going your way

Seeded to score 200+ points more than next team (similar to ASU 2024)

Bob's confidence came from:

  • Swam well at SECs on NOT a full taper
  • Top 4 scorers (Kós, Luke Hobson, Rex Maurer, etc.) were unshaved at SECs

Why Bob Gave Them More Rest Earlier

  1. Fall invite - needed to see where they were

  • ASU was confident but inexperienced at NCAAs
  • Texas had gotten 7th the previous year
  • Eddie Reese told Bob at breakfast: "I'm so sorry. They're way better than that."
  • Texas has to walk the hallways and see championship banners every day

"I was trying to coach them mentally as well as physically. If I just did a straight physiological plan, our invite I'd probably just rolled through it with most people, but I felt like we needed to see where they were and have them feel good about what we were doing so far." — Bob Bowman

Indiana Dual Meet: Going to the Well?

Ray Looze (Indiana coach): "You can only go to the well so many times."

Bob's response: They got TWO DAYS of fresher training before Indiana. Bob's philosophy: "I don't see the point in competing so far away from where you're going to be."

  • Warmed up the day before
  • No lifting (because Indiana flew in, so they didn't lift either)
  • "We saw the well over there somewhere. I don't know. You know what I mean? We kind of saw it."

Result: Way better than Bob expected.

"What's the point of swimming a 500 fifteen seconds slower than your best time? Maybe 5 or 10 seconds is good. But Michael [Phelps] was always relatively close to his speed and we trained 80,000 a week. When we went to a meet, we didn't just keep that going till the day we got there. It seems stupid to do that. Why would we even go? Just keep practicing." — Bob Bowman

The Championship Meet: Day-by-Day

Night 1: Amazing

Exceeded expectations. Very good.

Texas's advantage: First four events are their best events.

Day 2 Morning: Body Blows

  • 200 medley relay DQ in the final (every point counts)
  • Taking punches, just trying to hang in
  • But somehow still increased the lead slightly

Day 3 Morning (Last Prelims): Struggle

"We didn't swim as well as we needed to. A lot of that's inexperience. A lot of that was they'd given a lot in the first three days."

Bob's response: Gave them an objective assessment of what they needed to do.

The Emotional Control Lesson

Bob's first inclination: "All right, come on, we gotta—"

But that's wrong. They're already feeling the pressure. They don't need MORE pressure from the coach.

"The reality is if we want them to perform, they're already feeling the pressure. They don't need pressure from me. I need to take it away if anything. I need to mitigate it so they can perform at their best." — Bob Bowman

The seniors had a team meeting alone after Night 3. Whatever they said worked. The team hung in there.

"The team very much like the ASU team kind of motivated itself. We didn't have to give them too much." — Bob Bowman

Night 3 (Finals): The Comeback

What Texas needed:

  • A few people move up 3-5 places
  • Get point swings in their direction

Key performances:

  1. Luke Hobson - Moved up to 9th in 100 breast (substantial move)
  2. Nate Germonprez - Went from lane 1 to winning the consolation final (biggest difference maker)
  3. Jacob Welch - Moved up in platform diving (weren't sure they'd have a platform diver)

The 400 Free Relay:

  • Texas had to finish top 12
  • Had to be within a certain margin of Cal
  • Bob: "I would have liked to have had it done before the relay. At ASU we did that, then you can send them and they can do amazing times. But in our situation, we had to have everybody go."

Relay starts: "I said, be deliberate. Maybe not THAT deliberate, but it was great."

articles.title: Bob Bowman: Training Philosophy & Program Structure
articles.content:
# Bob Bowman: Training Philosophy & Program Structure

*Source: Podcast transcripts, practice observations (2024-2026)*

## Core Training Principles

### 1. The Process is Sacred

**Foundation**: "The process of success is more important than any outcome."

- Can't control how fast athletes swim
- Can't control pool conditions
- **CAN control**: How we prepare, standard of practice, daily attitude, modeling behavior

### 2. Consistency Over Perfection

**Long-term Development**:
- 20-year journey with Michael Phelps (age 11 → retirement)
- Programs don't change dramatically year-to-year
- "The inescapable reality of these training programs is they don't really change that much"
- Challenge: Keeping 3+ year athletes engaged with familiar program

### 3. Details Matter More Than You Think

**The 0.01 Second Philosophy**:
> "I bought a 37-acre horse farm and built a 5,000 square foot house off of that 100th of a second." - Phelps vs. Čavić, 2008

**Perfect Finish Training**:
- Every repeat is a chance to practice perfect finish
- Butterfly: fingers extended, horizontal, driving straight into wall
- Michael: 12 years of being yelled at about finishes
- Result: Muscle memory in high-pressure moment

## Program Structure

### Annual Periodization

**Components** (from presentation at ASU):
- Different training phases and types
- Strength training cycles
- Endurance training blocks
- Speed training phases
- Altitude training camps
- Competition schedule (major meets in red, lesser meets in blue/purple)
- Volume progression

**Visualization**: "It looks complicated but it's relatively detailed. I can look at that every day and say: Where are we? How are the kids doing? Does it look like we're on track?"

### Daily Practice Structure

#### Texas Implementation (2024-present)

**Key Difference from Eddie Reese**:
- **Eddie's approach**: Delivered sets verbally, lane by lane, time between sets
- **Bowman's approach**: Everything written on paper when athletes arrive, ~1 minute between sets
- "I try to make it less. They finish one set, I'll be like 'on the top, start the pulling.'"

**Initial Shock**:
- Texas swimmers completely "flummoxed" first few days
- Had to adapt to constant movement
- No downtime to process

#### Fall Morning Sessions

**Volume Focus**:
- 7,000-8,000 meters in 90-100 minutes
- General aerobic work: kicking, pulling, swimming
- Short rest intervals
- Building general fitness base

**Example Christmas Set** (Long Course Meters):
- 10 x 100 Free from dive, on 2:00 (maximal effort)
- Directly into: 10 x 50 Fly from dive, on 1:30 (maximal effort)
- Entire team (sprinters, distance, everyone)
- Climb out after every repeat
- Coaches yelling encouragement
- "Wow I don't even know if I can do this" → they do it → confidence grows

### The Quote System

**Every Practice Starts with a Quote**:
- Written at top of practice
- Sources: Twitter/X, stoic philosophers, athletic motivational accounts
- Sometimes dictated by available space on paper (shorter quotes on busy days)

**Example Quotes**:
- "Glory is fleeting but obscurity is forever." - Napoleon
- "Good judgment comes from experience. Most of experience comes from bad judgment." - Will Rogers
- Will Rogers quote use after major meet: teaching about learning from mistakes

## Building Discomfort & Resilience

### Progressive Pressure Training

**Concept**: Build tolerance for stress gradually

**Examples**:
1. **Minimal rest between sets** (low-hanging fruit)
2. **High-volume work** (uncomfortable but achievable)
3. **Maximal effort sets** (Christmas 10x100 + 10x50)
4. **Adversity practice** (goggles, equipment issues)

### Creating Uncomfortable Situations

**Michael Phelps Examples**:

1. **Flooded Goggles Training**:
   - Australia meet: Stepped on Michael's goggles (on purpose)
   - Michael swam prelim with leaking goggles
   - Bob: "Oh, that's crazy. I don't know how that could happen."
   - Built skill for when it happened in 2008 Beijing Olympics

2. **No Goggles Race**:
   - Michael didn't take goggles to blocks
   - Bob refused to bring them
   - Michael swam without them, won anyway
   - Built confidence and adaptability

**Philosophy**: "Don't try to make everything perfect for yourself. Be tough on yourself. You don't have to have a fancy pool."

### The "Underfunded YMCA" Mindset

> "This whole thing started with me and Michael in a pool that Time Magazine called an underfunded YMCA. We didn't need a lot. We just had some goals and some energy."

**Australian Coach Story**:
- Team complained water fountain wasn't cold enough
- Next day: No water fountain at all
- Lesson: Learn to deal with things

## Preparation Philosophy

### "Normal Performance in Abnormal Environment"

**Olympic Preparation Analogy**:
> "Could you hit a 3-foot putt in your backyard? Yes. Could you do it with family watching? Yes. Could you do it on Sunday at Augusta to win the Masters? Maybe. How about if you had to do that 3-foot putt on Sunday afternoon in the Masters in the middle of a hailstorm in the dark? That's like the Olympics."

**Focus**: Not teaching the skill, but managing yourself to execute the skill under crazy circumstances

### Practice = Performance Simulation

**What Can Be Rehearsed Daily**:
- Middle splits at race pace
- Perfect turns
- Race starts
- Specific warm-up routines
- Mental preparation sequence
- Putting on warmups like race day
- Thinking through race plan

**Cannot Simulate**:
- Swimming goal time during hard training
- Full taper performance
- Championship pressure

**Solution**: Rehearse components, build muscle memory, trust the process

## Training Adaptation & Individualization

### Michael vs. Leon

**Michael Phelps**:
- Thrived on relentless pressure
- High volume, consistent intensity
- Needed emotional activation
- Best when angry/motivated

**Leon Marchand**:
- Needs laid-back approach
- Same physical work, different emotional climate
- Performance suffers when too intense
- Best when calm, having fun

### College Athletes (Texas, 2024-25)

**Gradual Implementation**:
- Year 1: Started at 60-70% of full program
- Gradually increased over semester
- Respected Texas traditions while adding new elements
- Patient approach to buy-in

**Senior Leadership**:
- Bought in first
- Explained Bowman's system to teammates
- Helped translate expectations
- Self-regulated team meetings

## Work Capacity Building

### The Brain Muscle Concept

**Theory**: "There's a part of your brain that when you kind of get yourself to do something that you either don't want to do or you don't think you can do, every time you do that, a little area of your brain grows and gets better at it the next time."

**Application**:
- Find areas to build this "muscle"
- Consistent challenges, gradually increased
- Not always sledgehammer stress
- Small victories compound

### Aerobic Foundation

**Fall Focus**:
- General aerobic kicking, pulling, swimming
- Short rest
- High volume (7-8k mornings)
- Build work capacity before intensity

**Why It Works**:
- Athletes initially "not comfortable"
- After a while: "Oh yeah, I can do this"
- Confidence from volume capacity

## Competition Preparation

### Short Course Worlds / Mid-Season Meets

**Pro Athletes**:
- Treated like college mid-season invitational
- Little rest period
- Shave (for World Championships)
- Backup from invite (some athletes don't shave for invite, then shave for Worlds)

**College Athletes**:
- Strategic rest for confidence building
- Year 1 at Texas: More rest for invite than typical
  - Reason: New team needed confidence in program
  - Showed them it works
  - Future years: Less rest for invites

### Taper Philosophy

**Not Discussed in Detail in Transcripts**
- Focus on confidence building
- Trust the process
- Details matter more as meet approaches

## Handling Adversity in Training

### Michael Phelps 2008: Broken Hand

**Situation**: 6 months before Beijing Olympic Trials, Michael broke hand slipping on ice in Ann Arbor

**Decision Tree**:
- Option 1: Cast for 6 weeks
- Option 2: Surgery, swimming in 10 days
- **Choice**: "The surgery is tomorrow"

**Adaptation**:
- Worked through recovery
- Changed plans as needed
- "Everything was still possible, but we definitely had to change some things around"

**Principle**: "You've got to be prepared to adjust your plan"

## Standards of Practice

### What Bob Controls

1. **Standard of practice for the team**
2. **Standard he sets for himself as coach**
3. **How they approach practice**
4. **What happens when they walk in the door**
5. **Attitude expected from athletes**
6. **Behaviors he models daily**

### The Texas System (2024-present)

**Morning Practice Example**:
- Practice written on paper at pool deck
- Minimal time between sets
- High volume (7-8k in 90-100 min)
- Every repeat: opportunity for perfect technique

**Afternoon Practice**:
- More intense, specific work
- Race-pace sets
- Technical focus
- Quality over quantity

## Recruitment of Work Ethic

### The Big Swim Meet as Teaching Tool

**Indiana Dual Meet (2024)**:
- Used for confidence building
- More rest than optimal physiologically
- Mental coaching priority
- "They needed some confidence. I was trying to coach them mentally as well as physically."

**Result**: Strong seed times going into NCAAs, close performances to seeds

## Details Training

### Every Repeat Counts

**Butterfly Finish Example**:
- Correct: Fingers extended, horizontal plane, drive straight into wall
- Incorrect: Floating up, vertical, flat palm contact
- **Michael vs. Čavić**: Difference was 12 years of perfect finish training
- "Do it over. That finish was terrible. Make sure you're on a straight arm."

### The 21-Stroke Drill

**Preparation**: Michael knew he took ~21 strokes per 50 butterfly in practice

**Payoff**: 2008 Beijing, goggles filled with water, couldn't see
- Counted strokes instead of watching wall
- Won gold + World Record
- "It was not because Michael made some decision in that moment. You can't make a decision in that situation. You go with your instinct."

## Pro Group Training (Austin, 2024-present)

### Big Operation Management

**Size**: 10+ professionals + college team

**Staff Support**:
- Eric Posejpal (could be head coach anywhere)
- Trevor, Carol, Mitch
- "We work together to make all this happen"

**Athlete Responsibility**:
- Cannot do it for them
- Create environment where success can happen
- Athletes must own their swimming
- Independence required

### Professional Development Emphasis

**Modern Professional Behaviors** (encouraged):
1. **Dynamic warm-up routines** (Simone Manuel example)
2. **In-practice nutrition** (before, during, after)
3. **Hydration protocols**
4. **Recovery practices**
5. **Decision-making autonomy**

**Definition of Professional**:
> "Being a pro doesn't have anything to do with money. It means that you do your top quality of work when you don't feel like it. That's what professionals do. They come in and do the job every day to the best of their ability, regardless of the circumstances."

## Post-Olympic Training Adaptations (2024-present)

### Reagan Smith Example

**Approach**:
- Split time between Tempe (friends, social life) and Austin
- Not 100% intense training demands
- "A lot of fun for her to just have some freedom to just be a person and not a swimmer"
- World Cup tour as "fun training"
- Won't ramp to full program until ~March (9 months post-Olympics)

### Leon Marchand Example

**Transition Plan**:
1. Olympics (exhausting performance + aftermath)
2. Short break
3. World Cup tour (light training, fun, travel)
4. Another break
5. Skip Short Course Worlds
6. Australia with Dean Boxall (new stimulus, different environment)
7. Return to Austin refreshed (mental, physical, emotional)

**Philosophy**: "He's been in our program for 3 years. He'll need that to make the next step, but this journey to Australia is the perfect way to end his transition period."

## Training Tools & Resources

### Technology & Support

**Texas Resources**:
- Dietitians
- Sports psychologists
- Strength coaches
- "Whole army of people to help with choices"
- But: Athletes must follow through themselves

### Practice Writing

**Format**:
- Hand-written
- Quote at top
- Sets listed in order
- Minimal white space (to reduce procrastination)
- Clear, specific instructions

## Philosophy on Volume vs. Intensity

**Not Explicit in Transcripts but Implied**:
- Fall: Higher volume, aerobic focus
- Season progression: Shift toward intensity
- Respect for different phases
- Trust in periodization

## Key Principles Summary

1. **Process > Outcome** always
2. **Details matter** (perfect finishes, turns, technique)
3. **Discomfort = Growth** (get comfortable being uncomfortable)
4. **Consistency** (programs don't change much year-to-year)
5. **Individualization** (Michael ≠ Leon ≠ Texas team)
6. **Environment Creation** (success becomes inevitable)
7. **Athlete Ownership** (can't do it for them)
8. **Adaptability** (adjust plan when needed)
9. **Standards** (maintain high bar always)
10. **Patience** (20-year journeys, gradual implementation)

## Signature Bob Bowman Practices

1. **Minimal rest between sets**
2. **High-volume fall mornings** (7-8k in 90-100 min)
3. **Progressive pressure training**
4. **Adversity simulation** (broken equipment, difficult conditions)
5. **Perfect technique on every repeat**
6. **Written practices with quotes**
7. **Details, details, details**
8. **Professional behavior expectations**

articles.title: Taper & Peaking Strategies for Swimming
articles.description: When done right, taper can improve performance **2-6%**. At elite levels, that's the difference between 4th and gold.
articles.content:
# Taper & Peaking Strategies for Swimming

**Taper is the controlled reduction of training load to maximize performance at a target competition.**

When done right, taper can improve performance **2-6%**. At elite levels, that's the difference between 4th and gold.

---

## The Science of Taper

### What Happens During Taper

**Physiological adaptations:**
1. **Glycogen supercompensation** (muscle fuel stores increase)
2. **Muscle repair** (micro-damage from training heals)
3. **Hormonal rebalancing** (testosterone ↑, cortisol ↓)
4. **Neuromuscular freshness** (nervous system recovers)
5. **Blood volume normalization**
6. **Immune function improvement**

**Psychological benefits:**
- Reduced fatigue/staleness
- Increased motivation
- Improved focus
- Confidence from feeling fresh + fast

### Key Taper Variables

**1. Duration** (how long)  
**2. Volume reduction** (how much less)  
**3. Intensity** (how hard)  
**4. Frequency** (how often)

---

## Bob Bowman's Taper Philosophy

### The Core Principle

> "The process of success is more important than any outcome. I can't control how fast they're going to swim. I can't control the conditions in the pool. But what I can control is how we prepare for it and how we prepare for it every day for the last 6 months." — Bob Bowman

### Texas NCAA 2025 Taper

**Setup phase (SECs - 3 weeks before NCAAs):**
- Top 4 scorers (Kós, Hobson, Maurer) were **unshaved**
- Swam well but not fully rested
- Created **room to improve** for NCAAs

**Championship taper:**
- Full rest for top swimmers
- Kós went from losing every event at SECs → dominant at NCAAs
- 200 back: 1:34-low (Tuner's American Record broken by over a second)

### ASU 2024 Taper

**Bob gave more rest than usual for:**
1. Fall invite (needed to see where they were)
2. Mid-season dual meets (building confidence)

**Reason:** "I was trying to coach them mentally as well as physically."

**Result:** Team exceeded seeded times, won NCAA championship.

### The "Going to the Well" Debate

**Ray Looze (Indiana coach):** "You can only go to the well so many times."

**Bob's counter:**
> "I don't see the point in competing so far away from where you're going to be. What's the point of swimming a 500 fifteen seconds slower than your best time? Maybe 5 or 10 seconds is good. But Michael was always relatively close to his speed and we trained 80,000 meters a week. When we went to a meet, we didn't just keep that going till the day we got there. It seems stupid to do that. Why would we even go? Just keep practicing."

---

## Duration: How Long to Taper

### Research Guidelines

**Sprint events (50-100):** 1-2 weeks  
**Middle distance (200-400):** 2-3 weeks  
**Distance (800-1500):** 3-4 weeks

### Elite Practice

**Most common:** 2-3 weeks for championship meets

**Bob Bowman:** Typically 2.5-3 weeks for major championships

**Caeleb Dressel (Tokyo 2021):** 3-week taper, prioritized **sleep + recovery**

---

## Volume Reduction

### Research-Based Guidelines

**Week 1:** Reduce volume to **60-70%** of peak training  
**Week 2:** Reduce to **40-50%**  
**Week 3:** Reduce to **20-30%** (championship week)

### What to Reduce

**Cut first:**
- Aerobic base sets (long steady swimming)
- High-volume kicking
- Dryland volume (not intensity)

**Maintain:**
- Speed work
- Race-pace efforts
- Starts and turns
- Technical work

---

## Intensity: The Most Critical Variable

### The Golden Rule

**Reduce volume significantly. Maintain intensity.**

Intensity is what preserves speed and power. Volume creates fatigue.

### How to Maintain Intensity

**Keep:**
- Sprint sets (25s, 50s all-out)
- Race-pace work (goal pace for target events)
- Broken swims at race pace or faster
- Power work (explosive starts, underwaters)

**Example taper week:**
```
Monday AM: 3000m (50% volume) - includes 8x50 sprint @ 3:00
Monday PM: Dryland (30 min power focus)

Tuesday AM: 2500m - includes race-pace 200s
Tuesday PM: 2000m - speed + turns

Wednesday AM: OFF
Wednesday PM: 2500m - race simulation (prelims + finals)

Thursday: 2000m easy + starts

Friday: 1500m activation (easy + a few fast 25s)

Saturday: RACE
```

---

## Frequency: How Often to Train

### Research-Based

**Don't reduce training frequency** until final 3-5 days.

Reducing frequency too early can lead to detraining.

**Maintain:**
- Same number of sessions per week
- Just reduce duration and volume of each

**Final week:**
- Can drop to 1x/day or 5-6 sessions total
- Championship morning: 1000-1500m activation swim

---

## Psychological Taper

### Common Athlete Concerns

1. **"I feel slow/heavy"** (Days 3-7 of taper)
2. **"I'm losing fitness"**
3. **"Everyone else looks fast"**
4. **"I should be doing more"**

### Coaching Responses (Bob Bowman Style)

**Trust the process:**
> "We've done this hundreds of times. Your body is adapting. You'll feel it in 3-4 days."

**Focus on process goals, not outcome:**
- Don't think about winning/losing
- Think about: dive, breathe on second stroke, 1.2 tempo, head lower than hips into turn

**Rehearse success:**
- Visualization daily
- Watch videos of best swims
- Mental practice of race scenarios

---

## The "Reverse Taper" Phenomenon

**Days 3-7:** Many swimmers feel **worse** before feeling better.

**Why:**
- Body is rebalancing
- Nervous system adjusting
- Glycogen storage causes temporary heaviness
- Psychological: used to feeling tired = "normal"

**Solution:** Trust it. By days 8-10, the magic happens.

---

## Race Week Specifics

### 7 Days Out

- **Last hard workout** (race-pace work, but not all-out)
- After this: all speed + activation work

### 5 Days Out

- Suited swim (50-70% effort) to feel speed
- Broken swims at race pace or slightly faster

### 3 Days Out

- Short race-pace efforts (not full distance)
- Starts + turns + underwaters
- Total volume: <2000m

### 2 Days Out

- Easy 1500-2000m
- A few fast 25s to keep neuromuscular system firing
- Drills, technique work

### 1 Day Out

- **Championship morning:** 1000-1500m
- Easy swimming
- 4-6 fast 15s or 25s (not all-out, just sharp)
- Feel the water, wake up the nervous system

### Race Day Morning (Prelims)

- 1200-1800m warm-up
- Progressive: easy → moderate → race-pace 25s
- Specific to your event (fly/back/breast/free)

---

## Nutrition During Taper

### Carbohydrate Loading

**Days 3-1 before race:**
- Increase carb intake to **8-10g per kg bodyweight**
- Focus on easily digestible carbs (rice, pasta, bread, fruit)
- Reduce fiber/fat slightly to avoid GI issues

### Hydration

- Increase water intake
- Monitor urine color (pale yellow = hydrated)
- **Avoid alcohol** (dehydrates, disrupts sleep)

### Race Day

- **3-4 hours before:** High-carb meal (familiar foods)
- **1 hour before:** Banana, energy bar, or small snack
- **15 min before:** Sip water, maybe energy gel

---

## Common Taper Mistakes

### 1. Reducing Intensity Too Much

**Problem:** Swimmer gets slow because they never swim fast.

**Fix:** Maintain speed work throughout taper.

### 2. Not Reducing Volume Enough

**Problem:** Still too much fatigue, can't recover fully.

**Fix:** Be aggressive with volume cuts. It feels scary, but it works.

### 3. Adding New Workouts/Techniques

**Problem:** Body doesn't know what to do. Confusion, not adaptation.

**Fix:** Taper is NOT the time to experiment. Do what you know.

### 4. Over-Resting

**Problem:** Swimmer loses feel for water, gets sluggish.

**Fix:** Stay active. Reduce volume, but keep training.

### 5. Ignoring Sleep

**Problem:** All the taper in the world won't help if you're not sleeping.

**Fix:** Prioritize 8-9 hours/night during taper.

---

## Hubert Kós Case Study (Texas 2025)

### The Setup

**SECs (3 weeks before NCAAs):**
- Kós lost every event (50 back, 100 back, 200 back)
- Unshaved, not fully rested

**Bob's assessment:** "I really blew it with him last year at ASU. This year we got it right."

### The Taper

**Week before NCAAs:**
- Suited 150 backstroke: **1:10.0**
- Exactly what he'd split in the race

**Kós after that 150:** "Man, I could go like 1:33 or something."

**Bob:** "Yeah, feel free. Go ahead. Do it. Please do."

### The Result

**200 backstroke at NCAAs: 1:34-low**

- Broke Turer's American Record by over a second
- Kós was "mad he didn't go 1:33" (the goal they'd talked about)

**Bob:** "He's capable of that for sure."

**Outcome:** SEC Swimmer of the Year, but All-SEC Second Team (because you have to WIN an event to make first team - he lost every event at SECs).

---

## Michael Phelps: The 0.01-Second Finish

**2008 Beijing 100 Fly:** Beat Milorad Čavić by 0.01 seconds.

**Not a taper decision.** A **12-year ingrained habit.**

Every repeat in practice = chance to practice a perfect finish.

Bob yelling for 12 years: "Do it over. That finish was terrible. Make sure you're on a straight arm."

**Phelps:** Fingers fully extended, horizontal, driving straight into wall.

**Čavić:** Tired, floated up, touched vertical with flat hand.

> "I bought a 37-acre horse farm and built a 5,000-square-foot house off of that 1/100th of a second. I promise you it mattered." — Bob Bowman

---

## Individual Differences

### Some Swimmers Need More Rest

**Leon Marchand:** Needs long breaks, time off. After 2024 Paris: 6 weeks off.

**Training philosophy:** Always have **a little bit in reserve**. Never push to complete exhaustion.

### Some Swimmers Need Less Rest

**Michael Phelps:** Could go to the edge of exhaustion, be asked to do more, and be fine the next day.

**Training philosophy:** Give everything every day. When you don't have anymore, keep giving.

### How to Know

**Trial and error over seasons.**

Track:
- How they feel at different taper lengths
- Performance outcomes
- Athlete feedback

**Adjust accordingly.**

---

## The Mental Game of Taper

### Visualization Protocols

**Bob Bowman's system (used with Phelps):**

1. **Progressive relaxation** (get in relaxed state)
2. **Visualize race from two perspectives:**
   - First-person (in the water, feeling it)
   - Third-person (watching yourself from stands)
3. **Make it vivid:**
   - Smell the chlorine
   - See the venue
   - Feel the blocks under your feet
   - Hear the crowd
4. **Visualize multiple scenarios:**
   - Perfect race
   - Adversity (goggles leak, bad start, etc.)
   - How you'll respond to adversity

**Result:** By race day, you've swum it **300+ times** in your head.

### Process vs. Outcome Focus

**Outcome thoughts (create anxiety):**
- "I have to win"
- "What if I don't make finals?"
- "Everyone's watching"

**Process thoughts (create calm focus):**
- "Dive, breathe on second stroke, 1.2 tempo"
- "Head lower than hips into turn"
- "Drive fingers into wall, straight arm"

**Use process thoughts to control activation level.**

---

## Post-Taper: What's Next?

### After the Big Meet

**Option 1: Another meet in 2-4 weeks**
- Take 3-5 days very easy
- Rebuild volume gradually
- Mini-taper (1 week) before next meet

**Option 2: Off-season**
- Take 1-2 weeks completely off
- Active recovery (other sports, light swimming)
- Mental reset

**Option 3: Long course transition** (post-NCAAs → Olympic Trials)
- Bob Bowman: "Start long course immediately after short course. They're already in shape. We just roll right into it."
- Texas 2025: Top swimmers took **4 days off**, then back into full training

---

## Key Takeaways

✅ **Reduce volume aggressively (to 20-30% by championship week)**  
✅ **Maintain intensity (speed, race-pace, power)**  
✅ **Don't reduce frequency until final 3-5 days**  
✅ **Trust the process** (days 3-7 feel weird, then magic)  
✅ **Prioritize sleep** (8-9 hours/night)  
✅ **Visualize daily** (300+ mental reps before race day)  
✅ **Focus on process, not outcome**  
✅ **Individualize** (what works for Phelps won't work for Marchand)

---

## Links

- [Periodization Principles](./periodization-principles.md)
- [Bob Bowman: Texas NCAA Championship](../coaches/bob-bowman/texas-ncaa-championship.md)
- [Visualization Techniques](/wiki/mental/visualization-imagery.md)
- [Lactate Testing Protocol](./lactate-testing.md)
- [Race-Pace Training](./interval-training-systems.md)

Taper & Peaking Strategies for Swimming

Taper is the controlled reduction of training load to maximize performance at a target competition.

When done right, taper can improve performance 2-6%. At elite levels, that's the difference between 4th and gold.

The Science of Taper

What Happens During Taper

Physiological adaptations:

  1. Glycogen supercompensation (muscle fuel stores increase)
  2. Muscle repair (micro-damage from training heals)
  3. Hormonal rebalancing (testosterone ↑, cortisol ↓)
  4. Neuromuscular freshness (nervous system recovers)
  5. Blood volume normalization
  6. Immune function improvement

Psychological benefits:

  • Reduced fatigue/staleness
  • Increased motivation
  • Improved focus
  • Confidence from feeling fresh + fast

Key Taper Variables

1. Duration (how long)
2. Volume reduction (how much less)
3. Intensity (how hard)
4. Frequency (how often)

Bob Bowman's Taper Philosophy

The Core Principle

"The process of success is more important than any outcome. I can't control how fast they're going to swim. I can't control the conditions in the pool. But what I can control is how we prepare for it and how we prepare for it every day for the last 6 months." — Bob Bowman

Setup phase (SECs - 3 weeks before NCAAs):

  • Top 4 scorers (Kós, Hobson, Maurer) were unshaved
  • Swam well but not fully rested
  • Created room to improve for NCAAs

Championship taper:

  • Full rest for top swimmers
  • Kós went from losing every event at SECs → dominant at NCAAs
  • 200 back: 1:34-low (Tuner's American Record broken by over a second)

Bob gave more rest than usual for:

  2. Mid-season dual meets (building confidence)

Reason: "I was trying to coach them mentally as well as physically."

Result: Team exceeded seeded times, won NCAA championship.

Ray Looze (Indiana coach): "You can only go to the well so many times."

Bob's counter:

"I don't see the point in competing so far away from where you're going to be. What's the point of swimming a 500 fifteen seconds slower than your best time? Maybe 5 or 10 seconds is good. But Michael was always relatively close to his speed and we trained 80,000 meters a week. When we went to a meet, we didn't just keep that going till the day we got there. It seems stupid to do that. Why would we even go? Just keep practicing."

Research Guidelines

Sprint events (50-100): 1-2 weeks
Middle distance (200-400): 2-3 weeks
Distance (800-1500): 3-4 weeks
