Caeleb Dressel (Tokyo 2021): 3-week taper, prioritized sleep + recovery

Volume Reduction

Research-Based Guidelines

Week 1: Reduce volume to 60-70% of peak training
Week 2: Reduce to 40-50%
Week 3: Reduce to 20-30% (championship week)

What to Reduce

Cut first:

  • Aerobic base sets (long steady swimming)
  • High-volume kicking
  • Dryland volume (not intensity)

Maintain:

  • Speed work
  • Race-pace efforts
  • Starts and turns
  • Technical work

Intensity: The Most Critical Variable

The Golden Rule

Reduce volume significantly. Maintain intensity.

Intensity is what preserves speed and power. Volume creates fatigue.

How to Maintain Intensity

Keep:

  • Sprint sets (25s, 50s all-out)
  • Race-pace work (goal pace for target events)
  • Broken swims at race pace or faster
  • Power work (explosive starts, underwaters)

Example taper week:

Monday AM: 3000m (50% volume) - includes 8x50 sprint @ 3:00
Monday PM: Dryland (30 min power focus)

Tuesday AM: 2500m - includes race-pace 200s
Tuesday PM: 2000m - speed + turns

Wednesday AM: OFF
Wednesday PM: 2500m - race simulation (prelims + finals)

Thursday: 2000m easy + starts

Friday: 1500m activation (easy + a few fast 25s)

Saturday: RACE

Frequency: How Often to Train

Research-Based

Don't reduce training frequency until final 3-5 days.

Reducing frequency too early can lead to detraining.

Maintain:

  • Same number of sessions per week
  • Just reduce duration and volume of each

Final week:

  • Can drop to 1x/day or 5-6 sessions total
  • Championship morning: 1000-1500m activation swim

Psychological Taper

Common Athlete Concerns

  1. "I feel slow/heavy" (Days 3-7 of taper)
  2. "I'm losing fitness"
  3. "Everyone else looks fast"
  4. "I should be doing more"

Coaching Responses (Bob Bowman Style)

Trust the process:

"We've done this hundreds of times. Your body is adapting. You'll feel it in 3-4 days."

Focus on process goals, not outcome:

  • Don't think about winning/losing
  • Think about: dive, breathe on second stroke, 1.2 tempo, head lower than hips into turn

Rehearse success:

  • Visualization daily
  • Watch videos of best swims
  • Mental practice of race scenarios

The "Reverse Taper" Phenomenon

Days 3-7: Many swimmers feel worse before feeling better.

Why:

  • Body is rebalancing
  • Nervous system adjusting
  • Glycogen storage causes temporary heaviness
  • Psychological: used to feeling tired = "normal"

Solution: Trust it. By days 8-10, the magic happens.

  • Last hard workout (race-pace work, but not all-out)
  • After this: all speed + activation work

5 Days Out

  • Suited swim (50-70% effort) to feel speed
  • Broken swims at race pace or slightly faster

3 Days Out

  • Short race-pace efforts (not full distance)
  • Starts + turns + underwaters
  • Total volume: <2000m

2 Days Out

  • Easy 1500-2000m
  • A few fast 25s to keep neuromuscular system firing
  • Drills, technique work

1 Day Out

  • Championship morning: 1000-1500m
  • Easy swimming
  • 4-6 fast 15s or 25s (not all-out, just sharp)
  • Feel the water, wake up the nervous system

  • 1200-1800m warm-up
  • Progressive: easy → moderate → race-pace 25s
  • Specific to your event (fly/back/breast/free)

Fix: Stay active. Reduce volume, but keep training.

articles.content:
# Freestyle Breathing Technique

*Source: Bob Bowman - "What Swimmers Miss in Freestyle" (2024)*

## Core Principle: Head Moves WITH Body

> "In proper freestyle swimming, the head moves with the body to take the breath. It never moves independently." - Bob Bowman

### What NOT to Do

❌ **Lifting the chin** to take a breath  
❌ **Looking forward** during breath  
❌ **Looking to the side of the pool** during breath  
❌ **Over-rotating** the head  
❌ **"Checking the weather"** (looking up at ceiling)  

### What TO Do

✅ **Rotate head in line with body**  
✅ **Breathe in natural rotation of the body**  
✅ **One goggle in the water** during breath  
✅ **Head and body move together as one unit**  

## The Bow Wave Technique

### What is the Bow Wave?

When you swim, your head creates a bow wave (like a boat moving through water). This creates a small trough/pocket of air right next to your head.

**Proper breathing**: Take your breath INSIDE the bow wave
- One goggle stays IN the water
- About half your mouth shows above water
- You're actually breathing inside the pocket created by the bow wave

### Watch Michael Phelps

**What you'll see**:
- One goggle out of the water
- One goggle IN the water
- About half his mouth visible
- Breathing inside the bow wave
- Minimal head rotation
- Perfect alignment with body rotation

## Common Mistakes

### 1. Over-Rotation (Katie Ledecky Example)

**Problem**: Both goggles visible during breath
- Indicates over-rotation
- Head moving independently of body
- Creates drag and misalignment

**Fix**: Work on showing only one goggle during breath

### 2. "Checking the Weather"

**Problem**: Looking up toward ceiling when breathing
- Face is fully visible
- Over-rotating from the head
- NOT moving in unison with body

**Fix**: Keep rotation minimal, one goggle in water

### 3. Independent Head Movement

**Problem**: Head moving separately from body rotation

**Fix**: Lock head position to body, rotate as one unit

## Breathing Discipline

### Chase Kalisz Double-Breath Story

**The Problem**:
- Chase had habit of breathing into walls (double breath)
- Did it frequently in training
- Would carry into races

**The Intervention**:
- Bob: "Nuclear reaction" approach (admitted he went too far)
- Made Chase restart entire practice if he saw double breath
- Message: "Nobody's going to double breathe and win a gold medal"
- "If you want to get there, you have to do it"

**The Result**: 
- Chase never did another double breath
- Bowman: "I've never seen him do one since"

**Lesson**: Sometimes one decisive intervention on a bad habit is more effective than constant nagging

## Technical Checkpoints

### Self-Assessment Questions

1. **Can you see your whole face on video during breath?**
   - If yes → you're over-rotating

2. **Are both goggles visible when breathing?**
   - If yes → minimize rotation

3. **Does your head lift or look forward?**
   - If yes → work on lateral rotation only

4. **Can you feel the bow wave pocket?**
   - If no → you may be lifting too high

### Practice Drills (Implied)

**During warm-up** (30% of practice - Bowman's structure):
- Focus on gradual technique reinforcement
- Breathing drills with one goggle focus
- Body rotation drills
- Head position awareness

**During main set** (50% of practice):
- Maintain technique under fatigue
- Don't let breathing mechanics break down
- Every breath is practice for perfect technique

## Integration with Body Rotation

### The Complete Picture

**Freestyle breathing is NOT**:
- A separate action from swimming
- An interruption to your stroke
- Something your head does independently

**Freestyle breathing IS**:
- Part of natural body rotation
- Integrated into the stroke cycle
- Head locked to body, rotating together
- Minimal movement for maximum efficiency

### Timing

**When to breathe**: During natural body rotation
- As your body rolls to breathe side
- Head rotates WITH that roll
- Return rotation = head returns WITH body

## For Different Swimmer Levels

### Beginners
- Focus first on: keeping one goggle in water
- Drill: Side-kick with one arm extended, practice bow wave breathing
- Don't worry about speed, establish pattern

### Intermediate
- Refine: Minimizing rotation
- Video analysis: Check if both goggles showing
- Increase speed while maintaining technique

### Advanced
- Perfect: Bow wave breathing at race pace
- Integrate: Breathing every 2, 3, 5 strokes without technique breakdown
- Fatigue test: Maintain perfect breathing when exhausted

## Mental Cues

**"One goggle in the water"** - Primary focus cue

**"Head and body together"** - Unity cue

**"Breathe in the bow wave"** - Efficiency cue

**"No checking the weather"** - Correction cue

## Common Races & Breathing Patterns

**Sprint (50-100m)**:
- Minimal breathing or specific pattern
- Technique even more critical (less breathing = each breath must be perfect)

**Mid-Distance (200m)**:
- Consistent pattern (often every 2 or 3)
- Maintain technique through fatigue

**Distance (400m+)**:
- Regular breathing essential
- Technique must not break down over time
- Katie Ledecky: work on minimizing over-rotation even when tired

## Coaching Points (from Bowman's Approach)

### For Coaches

1. **Video is essential** - athletes often can't feel over-rotation
2. **Establish non-negotiables** - some habits need decisive intervention
3. **Practice = performance** - if they breathe wrong in practice, they'll breathe wrong in races
4. **Reinforce in warm-up** - 30% of practice is technique reinforcement time

### For Self-Coached Swimmers

1. **Film yourself regularly**
2. **Count goggles** - should only see one
3. **Feel the bow wave** - are you breathing in the pocket or above it?
4. **Check alignment** - is head rotation matching body rotation?

## The Bottom Line

**Proper freestyle breathing**:
- Head moves WITH body (never independently)
- One goggle in the water
- Breathe inside the bow wave
- Minimal rotation for maximum efficiency

**Impact**:
- Reduced drag
- Better body position
- More efficient energy use
- Faster swimming

**Remember**: Every breath in practice is practice for the perfect breath in your race. Make every rep count.

  • Don't worry about speed, establish pattern

Intermediate

  • Refine: Minimizing rotation
  • Video analysis: Check if both goggles showing
  • Increase speed while maintaining technique

Advanced

  • Perfect: Bow wave breathing at race pace
  • Integrate: Breathing every 2, 3, 5 strokes without technique breakdown
  • Fatigue test: Maintain perfect breathing when exhausted

Mental Cues

"One goggle in the water" - Primary focus cue

"Head and body together" - Unity cue

"Breathe in the bow wave" - Efficiency cue

"No checking the weather" - Correction cue

Common Races & Breathing Patterns

Sprint (50-100m):

  • Minimal breathing or specific pattern
  • Technique even more critical (less breathing = each breath must be perfect)

Mid-Distance (200m):

  • Consistent pattern (often every 2 or 3)
  • Maintain technique through fatigue

Distance (400m+):

  • Regular breathing essential
  • Technique must not break down over time
  • Katie Ledecky: work on minimizing over-rotation even when tired

Coaching Points (from Bowman's Approach)

For Coaches

  1. Video is essential - athletes often can't feel over-rotation
  2. Establish non-negotiables - some habits need decisive intervention
  3. Practice = performance - if they breathe wrong in practice, they'll breathe wrong in races
  4. Reinforce in warm-up - 30% of practice is technique reinforcement time

For Self-Coached Swimmers

  1. Film yourself regularly
  2. Count goggles - should only see one
  3. Feel the bow wave - are you breathing in the pocket or above it?
  4. Check alignment - is head rotation matching body rotation?

The Bottom Line

Proper freestyle breathing:

  • Head moves WITH body (never independently)
  • One goggle in the water
  • Breathe inside the bow wave
  • Minimal rotation for maximum efficiency

Impact:

  • Reduced drag
  • Better body position
  • More efficient energy use
  • Faster swimming

Remember: Every breath in practice is practice for the perfect breath in your race. Make every rep count.

articles.title: Masters Workouts: Progressive Speed & Descending Sets
articles.content:
# Masters Workouts: Progressive Speed & Descending Sets

*Source: Bayside/Great Neck Masters (2023-2024), 85 workouts parsed*

## Workout #3: Pyramid Negative Split (2300m SCM)

**Date**: 10/04/23 | **Location**: Bayside | **Focus**: Pacing control, negative splits

**Structure**:
- **Warmup**: 300 choice / 6x50 IM @ 1:00 (600m)
- **Main Set** (1600m):
  - 100 last 25 FAST @ 1:45 (rest 2:00)
  - 200 last 50 FAST @ 3:30 (rest 4:00)
  - 300 last 75 FAST @ 5:15 (rest 6:00)
  - 400 last 100 FAST @ 7:00 (rest 8:00)
  - **Then descend back down**:
  - 300 last 75 FAST @ 5:15 (rest 6:00)
  - 200 last 50 FAST @ 3:30 (rest 4:00)
  - 100 last 25 FAST @ 1:45 (rest 2:00)
- **Cool**: 100 EZ

**Key Features**:
- Teaches pacing control through fatigue
- Progressively longer distances with negative split focus
- Symmetrical pyramid structure
- Each repeat: coast first portion, crush final portion
- Intervals provide full recovery (almost 2:1 work:rest)

**Use When**:
- Teaching race pacing
- Building aerobic base with speed elements
- Developing negative split discipline
- Mid-season when fitness is solid

**Coaching Notes**:
- Watch for athletes crushing early portions (defeats purpose)
- "Last X meters FAST" means controlled swim, explosive finish
- Rest ratios allow full recovery = quality over volume
- Can modify distances/rest for different abilities

---

## Workout #9: Progressive Density 40x50 (2500m SCM)

**Date**: 10/25/23 | **Location**: Bayside | **Focus**: Speed endurance, progressive density

**Structure**:
- **Warmup**: 400 choice
- **Main Set**: 40x50 @ 1:00 (2000m)
  - **First 16**: Every 4th is FAST (4 fast out of 16)
  - **Next 12**: Every 3rd is FAST (4 fast out of 12)
  - **Next 8**: Every other is FAST (4 fast out of 8)
  - **Final 4**: ALL FAST (4 fast out of 4)
  - **Total FAST 50s**: 16 out of 40
- **Cool**: 100 EZ

**Key Features**:
- Progressive density of speed work
- Mental game: "only X more fast ones"
- Constant interval (1:00) = increasing fatigue
- Final block = pure grit (all 4 fast when exhausted)

**Use When**:
- Building speed endurance
- Mental toughness training
- Race simulation (holding speed when tired)
- Late season preparation

**Coaching Notes**:
- "FAST" means race pace effort, not all-out sprint
- Recovery 50s should be truly easy (don't let athletes cruise fast)
- Final 4 = test of will and conditioning
- Can adjust interval based on ability (:50/:55/1:00/1:05)

**Variations**:
- Use different strokes for fast 50s
- Make every 4th a different stroke
- Add fins for more speed
- Increase total to 50 or reduce to 30

---

## Workout #8: Ascending Ladder (2500m SCM)

**Date**: 10/23/23 | **Location**: Bayside | **Focus**: Aerobic threshold, pacing

**Structure**:
- **Warmup**: 300 choice
- **Pre-set**: 8x75 @ 1:30 (25 Drill/Drill/Build) (600m)
- **Main Set**: 2 rounds through:
  - 50 @ 1:00
  - 100 @ 2:00
  - 150 @ 3:00
  - 200 @ 4:00
  - 250 @ 5:00
  - **All FAST, All Choice** (1200m total)
- **Cool**: 100 EZ

**Key Features**:
- Ascending distances with proportional rest
- Constant pace challenge (can you hold your 50 pace for 250?)
- Choice of stroke = find what works when tired
- Two rounds = volume + pacing practice

**Use When**:
- Building aerobic capacity
- Threshold training
- Stroke versatility
- Testing pace sustainability

**Coaching Notes**:
- "All FAST" = threshold pace, sustainable but challenging
- Watch splits: should be relatively consistent per 50
- Stroke choice often changes round 1 → round 2 (fatigue)
- Can modify: all free, or assign stroke order

---

## Workout #11: Threshold Intervals - Hold Best (2450m SCM)

**Date**: 10/30/23 | **Location**: Bayside | **Focus**: Threshold pace, lactate tolerance

**Structure**:
- **Warmup**: 400 ROIM / 8x25 drill variations (600m)
- **Pre-set**: 3 rounds of 25 Scull / 50 DP-Tarzan / 75 Free @ intervals (450m)
- **Main Set**: 4 rounds of:
  - 3x100 Freestyle **Hold the Fastest Interval Possible**
  - Interval options: 1:15 / 1:20 / 1:25 / 1:30 / 1:35 / 1:40 / 1:45 / 1:50
  - **Goal**: Pick interval, hold all 3 at threshold pace
  - (1200m main)
- **Cool**: 100 EZ

**Key Features**:
- Self-selected interval = athlete ownership
- Must hold all 3 at same pace (no dying)
- 4 rounds = 12 total threshold 100s
- Challenge: Can you go faster interval in later rounds?

**Use When**:
- Threshold development
- Teaching pace judgment
- Building aerobic power
- Self-awareness training

**Coaching Notes**:
- First round: Find sustainable interval
- Rounds 2-3: Try to maintain or improve
- Round 4: All-out effort to hold fastest interval
- Track best interval round to round for progress

**Variations**:
- Assign specific interval for whole team
- Make it 4x100 instead of 3x100
- Add stroke requirement
- Incorporate negative split requirement

---

## Workout #14: Fast/Easy Contrast (2200m SCM)

**Date**: 11/08/23 | **Location**: Bayside | **Focus**: Speed contrast, recovery management

**Structure**:
- **Warmup**: 200 Swim / 200 Kick / 200 IM Drill (600m)
- **Pre-set**: 4x150 @ 3:00 (25 Build FAST / 50 Flutter Kick / 75 Free) (600m)
- **Main Set**: 3 rounds of:
  - 3x50 (FAST / EZ / FAST) @ 1:30
  - 6x25 (EZ / FAST) @ :35 / :30 / :25 (progressively tighter)
  - (900m main)
- **Cool**: 100 EZ

**Key Features**:
- Contrast training: fast then easy in same set
- Progressive interval tightening on 25s
- Teaches body to recover while still moving
- Quality speed work with built-in recovery

**Use When**:
- Speed development
- Recovery swimming skills
- Pace contrast training
- Peak season sharpening

**Coaching Notes**:
- "FAST" = sprint effort, not threshold
- "EZ" = truly easy, shake out lactic acid
- Interval tightening on 25s = less rest, same speed
- Final 25s @ :25 = minimal rest, test of recovery

---

## Workout #5: Kick Progression (2200m SCM)

**Date**: 10/14/23 | **Location**: Great Neck | **Focus**: Kicking endurance, mental toughness

**Structure**:
- **Warmup**: 300 choice
- **Main Set** (Progressive volume):
  - 100 Kick FAST @ 2:00
  - 8x50 Free @ :45/:50/:55 (athlete choice)
  - 200 Kick FAST @ 4:00
  - 8x50 Free @ :45/:50/:55
  - 300 Kick FAST @ 6:00
  - 8x50 Free @ :45/:50/:55
  - (1800m main)
- **Cool**: 100 EZ

**Key Features**:
- Progressive kick distance (100 → 200 → 300)
- Same free recovery set between
- Tests leg endurance and mental resolve
- Proportional rest (2:1 ratio)

**Use When**:
- Building kick strength
- Developing leg endurance
- Mental toughness sessions
- Correcting weak kick

**Coaching Notes**:
- "FAST" kick = race pace kick with board
- Free 50s are recovery, not cruise
- Watch for technique breakdown on 300 kick
- Can add fins for power emphasis

---

## Workout #15: Aerobic Repeats with Kick Emphasis (2500m SCM)

**Date**: 11/11/23 | **Location**: Bayside | **Focus**: Aerobic base, kick integration

**Structure**:
- **Warmup**: 300 choice
- **Pre-set**: 2 rounds of:
  - 2x75 Drill variations @ 1:30
  - 2x75 Fly/Back/Breast @ 1:30
  - 2x75 Kick on Back @ 1:30
  - (900m)
- **Main Set**: 6 rounds of:
  - 50 Kick w/ Board FAST @ 1:30
  - 3x50 Free Aerobic @ :40/:45/:50 (choice)
  - (1200m main)
- **Cool**: 100 EZ

**Key Features**:
- Kick blast then aerobic swimming
- Self-selected intervals on free
- High volume aerobic work
- Kick stays constant while free intervals can adjust

**Use When**:
- Building aerobic base
- Kick-swim integration
- Early season volume
- Technique reinforcement

**Coaching Notes**:
- Fast kick = max effort
- Aerobic free = sustainable, no lactic acid
- Watch for athletes choosing too tight interval
- 6 rounds = significant volume, pace accordingly

---

## Common Themes in Progressive Sets

### Structure Patterns

1. **Pyramid** (up then down)
2. **Ladder** (ascending or descending only)
3. **Density** (increasing frequency of fast)
4. **Contrast** (fast/easy alternating)
5. **Volume** (progressive distance)

### Interval Strategies

- **Proportional rest**: Distance doubles, rest doubles (100@2:00, 200@4:00)
- **Constant interval**: Same rest regardless of distance
- **Tightening interval**: Progressively less rest
- **Self-selected**: Athlete chooses sustainable interval

### Speed Definitions

- **FAST** = Race pace threshold effort (sustainable)
- **MAX/BEST** = All-out sprint effort
- **EZ** = True recovery pace
- **Aerobic** = Sustainable training pace below threshold

### Progression Methods

**By Volume**:
- 100 → 200 → 300 → 400 (ascending)
- 400 → 300 → 200 → 100 (descending)
- 100 → 200 → 300 → 200 → 100 (pyramid)

**By Density**:
- Every 4th fast → every 3rd → every 2nd → all
- 1 fast per round → 2 → 3 → 4

**By Rest**:
- :45 → :40 → :35 → :30 (tightening)
- Full recovery → moderate → short (fatigue training)

## Programming Notes

**Early Season**: Use longer rest, lower density, focus on technique
**Mid-Season**: Moderate rest, building density, maintaining technique under fatigue
**Late Season**: Shorter rest, high density, race simulation

**Recovery Indicators**:
- Can hold times round to round
- Technique stays solid
- Splits remain consistent
- Athlete feels challenged but not destroyed

**Red Flags**:
- Times falling apart mid-set
- Technique breakdown
- Unable to complete prescribed work
- Excessive soreness next day

## Adaptations by Ability Level

### Beginner Masters
- Longer rest ratios
- Fewer total reps
- More recovery swim
- Focus on finishing

### Intermediate Masters
- Standard rest as written
- Full volume
- Some stroke variety
- Building speed endurance

### Advanced Masters
- Tighter rest ratios
- Add volume (extra rounds)
- Stroke-specific work
- Racing simulation

## Sample Week Structure

**Monday**: Aerobic base (Workout #15 style)
**Wednesday**: Threshold intervals (Workout #11 style)
**Friday**: Speed/density (Workout #9 style)
**Saturday**: Volume pyramid (Workout #3 style)

**Total weekly meters**: ~8000m
**Quality sessions**: 2-3
**Recovery sessions**: 1-2

## Key Takeaways

1. **Progressive sets teach pacing** under increasing fatigue
2. **Density training** builds speed endurance
3. **Pyramid sets** develop both pacing and mental toughness
4. **Contrast work** teaches recovery while moving
5. **Self-selected intervals** build athlete ownership

**Remember**: The point isn't to survive the set. It's to execute the set with purpose, pace awareness, and technical excellence.

# Sprint & Speed Training Sets (Masters)

**30 high-intensity workouts focused on maximum speed, power, and race pace.**

All workouts from Bayside Masters (SCM course). Use these when targeting:
- Sprint events (50-100)
- Starts and turns power
- Maximum velocity training
- Race-specific speed

---

## 09/30/23 - 2000m - Sprint Kick Focus

**Main Set:**
- 12x50 Fly/BK, BK/Breast, Breast/Free @ 1:00
- 5x100 Free Best Average @ 3:00
- **8x25 Flutter Kick FAST @ 1:00** ← Sprint emphasis

**Use When:** Building kick power for sprints

---

## 10/02/23 - 2200m - Underwater Sprint Work

**Main Set:**
- 4x{125 (100 IM + 25 Fly) @ 2:30, 75 Back or Breast @ 1:30}
- **16x25 Sprint w/ fins @ 1:00**
  - Odd: Underwater kick
  - Even: Choice

**Use When:** Maximizing underwater speed and power

---

## 10/04/23 - 2300m - Negative Split Speed

**Main Set (Descending Fast Finishes):**
- 100 last 25 FAST @ 1:45
- 200 last 50 FAST @ 3:30
- 300 last 75 FAST @ 5:15
- 400 last 100 FAST @ 7:00

**Use When:** Training sprint finish + race pacing

---

## 10/14/23 - 2200m - Kick + Free Sprint Combo

**Main Set:**
- 100 Kick FAST @ 2:00
- 8x50 Free @ :45/:50/:55 (descending intervals)
- 200 Kick FAST @ 4:00
- 8x50 Breast Descend to FAST @ 1:00
- 100 Kick FAST

**Use When:** Sprint conditioning with kick emphasis

---

## 10/18/23 - 2100m - Stroke-Specific Sprint Progression

**Main Set:**
- 12x25 Fly @ :35
- 6x50 Back @ 1:00
- 3x100 Breast @ 2:00
- 6x25 Free Sprint @ :30

**Use When:** Multi-stroke sprint development

---

## 10/21/23 - 2200m - Descending Sprint Freestyle

**Main Set:**
- 8x50 Free Descend 1-4 @ :50
- 4x75 Free Descend 1-2 @ 1:15
- 2x100 Free FAST @ 2:00
- 4x25 Free Descend to FAST @ :30

**Use When:** Building progressive sprint speed

---

## 10/23/23 - 2500m - Power + Speed Combo

**Main Set:**
- 10x100 Free @ 1:30 (Hold fastest possible)
- 8x25 Sprint Choice @ :45

**Use When:** Threshold speed into sprint power

---

## 10/30/23 - 2450m - Tarzan Drill + Speed

**Main Set:**
- 3x{25 Scull @ :30, 50 DPS/Tarzan @ 1:00, 75 Free @ 1:30}
- 4x{3x100 Hold Fastest Interval @ varying intervals}

**Use When:** Technique refinement + speed maintenance

---

## 11/01/23 - 2300m - Sprint IM Development

**Main Set:**
- 8x75 IM @ 1:30
- 6x50 Free Negative Split @ 1:00
- 8x25 Choice FAST @ 1:00

**Use When:** IM sprinters, multi-stroke speed

---

- 6x25 ALL OUT @ :45

**Use When:** Maximum sprint volume + intensity

---

## Coaching Notes

### Sprint Training Principles

**1. Full recovery between reps**
- Sprint = maximum effort requires maximum recovery
- 1:3 to 1:5 work:rest ratio for true sprints

**2. Quality > Quantity**
- 8 perfect 25s >> 16 mediocre 25s
- Maintain excellent technique even when fatigued

**3. Race-specific pacing**
- Practice target race pace + slightly faster
- Build database of what pace feels like

### Common Sprint Set Structures

**Short sprints (25s):**
- 8-16 x 25 @ :30-1:00
- Fins optional (adds power + reduces stroke count)

**Mid sprints (50s):**
- 6-12 x 50 @ :45-1:00
- Descending intervals build intensity

**Long sprints (75-100s):**
- 4-8 x 75-100 @ 1:15-2:00
- Best average or descending format

### Integration with Training Cycle

**Base phase:** 1 sprint session/week  
**Build phase:** 2 sprint sessions/week  
**Peak phase:** 2-3 sprint sessions/week  
**Taper:** Maintain sprint volume, reduce total volume
